critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: Using the 90-day maturity window for the challenger and 30-day for the baseline reflects a reasonable improvement in data infrastructure.
Why wrong: Comparing PR-AUC values computed against different label standards is comparing different quantities. The target variable itself changed between evaluations. A model's PR-AUC against 90-day labels and the same model's PR-AUC against 30-day labels are not directly comparable because 90-day labels contain additional true-fraud cases not present at 30 days. The improvement in data infrastructure is a valid operational advance, but the benchmark comparison must use the same label standard for both models or the comparison is confounded.
Evidence: Apply 90-day labels to the Q1 baseline evaluation and recompute its PR-AUC. If baseline PR-AUC rises materially when evaluated with 90-day labels, the mismatch explains the gap.

Issue 2
Claim: Separate quarterly evaluations are sufficient for a model comparison.
Why wrong: Even if label maturation were matched, different quarters after a merchant onboarding campaign introduce distribution shift. Q2 has a different transaction size and geography mix than Q1. A model that exploits merchant-geography interaction features may appear stronger in Q2 simply because Q2 has more merchants with favorable feature signatures for that model, not because it is fundamentally better.
Evidence: Score the Q1 transaction sample with the challenger model to compare baseline and challenger PR-AUC on the same Q1 data with matched labels.

Issue 3
Claim: The six-basis-point gain is large enough that statistical uncertainty does not matter.
Why wrong: Fraud prevalence of approximately 0.2% means the positive class is very sparse. PR-AUC is sensitive to small changes in the ranking of rare events. At typical fraud dataset sizes, a 0.006 PR-AUC difference can be within the 95% confidence interval under bootstrap resampling. Without quantified uncertainty, the claim that this gain is reliably non-zero is unfounded.
Evidence: Bootstrap the PR-AUC difference on a common evaluation window to determine whether the 95% CI excludes zero.
defender_raw: The 90-day label standard is a genuine improvement in label quality and it makes sense to evaluate the challenger against the best labels available. The temporal split by week is the appropriate fraud evaluation design. The PR-AUC gain of 0.006 is small but the fraud operations team has empirical experience with the relationship between PR-AUC and investigator workload. A matched comparison is the right next step to confirm the gain, but the directional result is not implausible.
adjudication_raw: The defender concedes that a matched comparison is needed. The defense's strongest point is that the directional signal is 'not implausible' — but the critique's point is that it is not validated. On Issue 1: applying 90-day labels to the Q1 baseline is exactly the right remediation and the defender implicitly accepts this is needed. On Issue 2: the defender does not address the merchant onboarding population shift. On Issue 3: the fraud team's experience with PR-AUC is not a substitute for statistical quantification. All three issues stand. Critique wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: comparing PR-AUC against different label maturation standards is comparing different quantities
Issue 2: merchant onboarding campaign between Q1 and Q2 creates distribution shift confound
Issue 3: six-basis-point gain at rare prevalence requires bootstrap CI to be interpretable

empirical_test.condition: Apply 90-day matured labels to the Q1 baseline evaluation; score Q1 transactions with the challenger model; compute paired bootstrap 95% CI for the PR-AUC difference on matched Q1 data
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Matched PR-AUC gap falls below 0.003 or the 95% CI crosses zero
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Matched gap is at least 0.006 with 95% CI excluding zero
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Positive gap above zero but below 0.006 with CI barely overlapping zero
